speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 21, 1995, tape number 2. And we are interviewing Mr. Leo Pop. Mr. Pop, please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just left off where I came to the others with-- in Heerlen with Miss Johanna Hookstat. She is the one who took me into her house, being a single lady. I was told that if I were asked, I was one of the nephews from the city of Rotterdam that was bombed out during-- at the beginning of the war. A lot of those kids were, you know, put down, not hidden as such, all-- you know, moved all over the country in order to, you know, to basically survive the war, since all the homes were all bombed. Like I mentioned before, this lady lived on the second floor. Here sister and-- and brother-in-law lived downstairs with three kids-- two boys and a girl. The boys were basically-- the oldest boy was more or less my age-- a few years younger. And I just-- I had a normal life with them while, of course, in the beginning, I was very sad. You know, there was-- And I was told, oh, it's just for a few, you know, few weeks. The war, you know, the war will be over. And be-- and you can go back home to your parents. I kind of like [? imagined ?] the underground was quite often in and out of the house. They told me to write a letter, which-- [? what ?] they would take to my parents, which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the letter said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wrote them a letter that I was, you know, wanted to come home and all this. And, you know, all-- all basically this. And then, I don't know, a week later or so, I got a letter back from the [INAUDIBLE] underground, what wasn't supposed to be from my parents. I-- the way the letter is written, looking at it now-- plus, I asked my surviving aunt in Amsterdam if that-- if she remembered this writing of my father. And she says, no, it's not his.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most likely, that is a lot of what is made up by the underground, answering my questions, basically, and just stating that, you know, keeping-- keep on hanging in there. You know, it soon it will be over. And you'll be together again. And that was from my father, actually, who wrote a letter. As it turned out, I was put down in the-- in the home or taken away and put in my hiding spot around the end of June.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, as far as I know from the information, got killed on July the 16th in the same year. So I don't think they ever got the letter and know that this letter was written by them. So this was all done in 1943. What I heard after the war is my parents, being still left in the house, [? what ?] had so-called the seals on the doors. They couldn't go out on the street anymore because nobody was supposed to know they still living there. One of the neighbors in the back balcony looked after the food, brought the food over. It was observed, again, by one of the other neighbors around. And he kind of informed the Germans. And-- and one day, the whole house-- the Germans was on the roof all over-- they had gone through the neighbors, sat on the roof. And someone knocked on the door. And my mother comes out. And there they were. And they were just picked up and put in transport. And-- that's what I heard after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the first day you went to this woman's apartment? Do you remember what she said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really as such. Well, I-- they tried to make me real welcome. I know there were the people downstairs-- and she, of course. And, you know, it was a small house, basically. And so there wasn't all that much room. There was a lot of people live there. But nevertheless, I-- you know, I-- from then on, I started to become familiar with the boys there. And I kind of later on grew up with them as such. They had a big garden. You know, and-- you know they didn't go to school because I guess they-- especially one boy was still younger. So he didn't have to go to school yet. I didn't go to school either. I hadn't gone to school since '41, '42. But as fortunate as it happened, it was in July '43 that I got there. In September '44, on the 17th, we were liberated by the Americans. So the duration was just over a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that year, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I kind of, you know, went-- according-- went along with whatever they did. I mean, on Sundays, we went to church, because they were quite religious people. And being just a nephew from Rotterdam, well, you know, you-- I went to church with them. I could not go to school because that was, you know, too dangerous. And the schools were, you know-- were the-- you know, Christian schools. They didn't even let you in, [? and that's ?] because that would put everybody in danger. So I did not go to school. And kind of during the school hours, I more or less stayed in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you taught by anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, no. And then after, you know, the kids came out of school, I could play outside. So, and that's what we did-- I kind of basically grew up a little bit, although you knew, you know, your brother was somewhere. My-- my niece was somewhere, I knew that. I know-- you didn't know where your parents were, but you know. And then the underground, and-- but then, of course, they had all kind of rules too. Even for the normal people, you had to be in at 7 o'clock. And you couldn't go out after that so many hours. And they came through the streets. And we knew that couple hours is over, there were people who also were collaborating so-called with the-- with the Germans. But nevertheless, as it turned out, they never even noticed or informed the Germans as such.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you baptized?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was baptized later on. That was way back, way later. But, you know, being eight years old, I was kind of brought into the Christian faith at that time. Grew up, and you know, from there, after the war, I stayed with the-- those people as well. And can-- went to Christian school when the war was over in 1945. And from there, I went to high school and so on. But that was all [? so ?] Christian. And basically, you didn't know better. And I was baptized. But fortunately, I am [INAUDIBLE] now. And, you know, I consider myself Jewish again. Although, I'm not going to synagogue now. But I feel Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go back to that time? What did you call the woman that cared for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt. She-- I called her aunt. And still, as of today, she's still aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She treated me fairly well. There, again, and this is a point what really I found out myself, and I later on got married and had children. I have missed a father person. I mean, she did OK on her own. But she was a single girl. What does she really know how to lead and guide you? And I've missed that myself. And when I had my own three sons, I really could see what-- what-- you know, maybe-- I tried to do my best as a father for my own sons. And then I could see what I really had missed myself because of the war as it turned out to be, but-- you know, she-- she tried on her own way. I mean, you know, there's no-- but just a single lady. You know. I have lost, you know, quite a bit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any close calls with the Gestapo at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- no, not really. No, the only one when I stayed in Amsterdam where we-- and, you know, once in a while when I was still in Amsterdam, that, you know, they came. Because you had to wear the star. And so you kind of stayed away from everything. And even before the star came out, they came up quite often, you know. Are you Jewish? And if you were-- because that's what they did. They just picked up whoever they could find and didn't even tell their parents or nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about when you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when I was in hiding, no. There-- although like I say, the people-- some people, they-- they suspected it. They didn't know for sure. And there were quite a few, more or less, Dutch people who collaborated with the Germans living in the street. Fortunately, it never-- and you know, because it was also dangerous, just like I mentioned. The underground came into the house quite often in all times of the day and night. And you know, sometimes I never knew who I was sleeping with in the bed upstairs because they came in sometime, and, you know, they just left again early in the morning and went to-- had to go to Amsterdam or another city, pick up some [? other ?] Jewish kids and-- so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the town like that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't-- really it was in the coal mine district. They had the coal mines. And it was, I would say, really relatively rich town. You know, it was a smaller town. But everybody was doing quite well, if you want to call it that during the war, of course. But then again, nobody was allowed radios, and all this, and motorcycles, or whatever it was. No, you-- Because I know the gentleman downstairs, what I call uncle, uncle and aunt also downstairs. They-- he had a radio. And he had them in a workshop. What-- in the back, you know, and you just had to turn it on. And-- and then you could hear the news from England, because, you know, that was-- the real news, what you could hear. Otherwise, it was all German propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing any of the broadcasts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I remember, you know, they-- sometimes you could hear it. You know, we sat around, come and listen. And, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, you could hear-- it was-- they had a program. What-- it always started with But, you know, that's when they-- you knew they were coming on. And they would had-- and, you know, telling they had bombed that city again. And they were coming this. And they were at the invasion in '44, in France there. We-- we-- we heard all about that. And it didn't take too long before we were liberated, because the Americans came quite fast up to that province, the southern part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the day you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was on a Sunday, September the 17th, 1944. The whole day we heard rumors that the Americans were on the outskirts. And they were just-- you know, they were just nearing the city. We could see all the Germans go back up on bicycles, on horse, and whatever it was. And they started to move out, because the city of Heerlen is basically, I would say, 10 miles from the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all those Germans went back to their own-- to the [INAUDIBLE]. And, you know, we could see them all go back and all this. And then, finally, at, I guess, 6 o'clock or 5 o'clock at night, we got-- you know, while they hi in the city. And, you know, so we are free. I know we had the-- although it was not even allowed, you recall in those little flowers-- the African-- no, not the African. What do you call it? The orange flowers-- marigolds-- marigolds that is, in the orange.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would go. And everybody put orange, because that's the color of the Dutch-- you know, the orange, the House of Orange. So everybody was wearing marigolds, the orange. And that's-- we went to see them. And, you know, you that-- you couldn't believe that was the Americans. But then, of course, you know, right after, I said, well, now my parents will be back. Well, as it turned out, it wasn't. But, you know, we didn't know. Because the war was not over yet. It was just '44. It wasn't too long after that I-- the underground brought me back to-- brought me in contact with my brother, again, because he was also [INAUDIBLE] liberated. So we saw each other from time to time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was he living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was living also with an-- with an older lady just by herself. And, you know, and my niece was also in-- in the same town where my brother was. Well, this may  be 20 miles from where I was. But all in the southern part of Limburg, and we were all more or less liberated within a week. So then after, the underground kind of brought us together. But then of course, the rest of the country was still at-- at war. And it didn't-- and it lasted till 1945 till they finally surrendered in the northern part. But, you know, where I was, we were liberated in '44. Of course, the Americans moved into, you know, the schools. So we couldn't go to schools. We had Americans coming by. They had their kitchens and everything in the back yards. And it-- they passed us all around. And the guns were there. And, you know, it was really a different world because of-- of that. But. Well, you know, so that's how we kind of got to know the Americans. And the next day, of course, we saw the American infantry going up-- back to-- you know, go ahead to Germany. And, you know, it was not-- it was great to that all. And of course, you know, I-- I always, you know, was hoping, well, once the war is over, you are told, well, it is just this part of the country liberated. Wait till the war ends. Then your parents will be back, but.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your aunt, the woman that looked after you, do in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, really, she stayed home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to help look for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because really I couldn't do much at that time yet, because it was still out-- you know, it's just the part where we lived was liberated. And as it turned out, her brother who was in-- in the military at the beginning of the war was also hidden. And he had two daughters. And they came to live also with my aunt-- with that aunt I lived with. So it was really crowded all around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, fun. I guess if you want to call it, basically, probably-- normal, tried to be a normal life, if you want to call it that. You know, I got some friends. And-- and all this. You know, but kind of not in church all the time too. Then, again, in  '44, I was, you know, 9, 10 years old. So, you know, you still missed your parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then of course, as you probably know, the-- the rest of Holland still had to [? bet ?] 1945 winter coming. And they had no food, nothing, and all this. However, finally in 1945, in May, the Germans surrendered. And it wasn't long after that that my aunt, not the one I lived with, but the sister of my mother, who had moved up to the northern part of the country in Friesland, in the hiding place, that she managed to get a list of surviving Jews. That's why we had to register with the police after we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the lists were all there, what stayed the Jewish people. And she got hold of a list in her part of the country and found that I-- we survived. So didn't take her long to come down-- whatever ways. There was no transportation, nothing. But she managed to come down to my place, and, you know, with my brother and my niece. And then she says, well, now we're gonna wait till your parents come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her parents-- my grandparents. And, well, they just didn't come back. And the only one who came back was her brother, a younger brother, that got married during the war, when I mentioned before maybe, when we had-- you know, in the synagogue, in Portuguese synagogue in Amsterdam. It was in '41, '42 that he got married, in '42, where we had to wear the Star of David. And he came back with his wife, you know, looking like, you know, thin and nothing-- you know, no clothing on. You know. And they even had a child. They were-- [INAUDIBLE] put down to Theresienstadt. And she gave birth to a baby there. And they had the child with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the only one who really came back from the concentration camp. Later on, it wasn't too long after, that also a brother of my father came back. He survived the concentration camp. The mines, he worked in the mines in Poland. His wife and his two girls all died there. But he survived. And he came back also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then another uncle, a brother of my father, he came out. And he also-- what we found out that he was, also had found a hiding place in another part of the country. So as it turned out, I had after the war, two brothers of my father. One came back. One was hiding. And of my mother's side, her-- her sister was hiding and her brother who survived Theresienstadt. That was all that was left of the family. And now my aunt, in Amsterdam, she often said, well, it was all-- you know, no money, nothing was around. But she says now quite often, I should have moved with you and your brother and my niece to Israel right after the war, as it turned out well. I am the only one who stayed with the people I was hidden with during the war. My brother was put in a Jewish orphanage because he was younger. And my niece was placed with some other people again too. And [INAUDIBLE]. But I was the only who stayed with the people I went to in '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become adopted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not so much-- well, adopted. I guess, eventually, it went to court, because, you know, it was all during-- till it finally straightened out. And she was appointed as my guardian or whatever it is-- yeah, by the courts in '48. So that's when I could stay there. It was around '48. And my aunt in Amsterdam, she gave permission to the court that I should stay there. Plus the-- you know, but she kind of stipulated that I should not be converted to the Jewish faith-- to the Christian faith. But, you know, as it turned out, I was baptized and all this. But, you know, that's beside the point. So. But then later on, you started to get information from the Red Cross and so on that, people just had gone to that concentration camp and never returned. So I lost quite a few nephews, nieces, uncles, aunts, grandparents who never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever talk to you about what happened? Did she try to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no, because I don't know. She didn't know what, of course, has happened. And for one reason or another, no, it was never talked about. The only one I could talk about it was just my aunt in Amsterdam who, you know, was, of course, my sister's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Doche, ?] Dorothea. And, you know, she is still alive today. She is about 83, 84 now. So. Yeah, That's the-- my uncle, her brother, then who came back from Theresienstadt, he has-- he died in the meantime on heart attack. And so my other uncle, who came from Poland from the camp, he also, you know, passed away. But that was, you know, after the war, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life from 1948 on.

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1928 on? Well, like I say, I finally went on to school in Heerlen in 1945, I guess, when the Americans more or less, you know, gave the school-- made them available again. I went to a Christian school, where I went into grade-- I guess grade four or five. So I missed all the years in between. And-- but nevertheless, I kind of made up whatever. And from there, I went to high school. After high school, I went to university and studied mechanical engineering. I graduated there in '58. And, of course, as it was, in '58, you had to, you know, enlist in the army. Not in 1958. I had already, when you turned 18, what was earlier, of course. Then I had to enlist and get into the army. But because I was studying, I got delay, and you know, I got-- every year, another year until my studies was finished. I finished in '58 in May. And I had to call to enlist myself in the army in August. So it didn't give me all that much time. And I had kind of more or less decided I didn't want to go in the army. It's not where-- you know, because having-- in university, you probably had to go in the officer's training. But I just didn't like. So I had decided to move to Canada. Those people, I should mention, also who lived in Heerlen downstairs, they had moved to Canada in 1954. So my aunt, when I lived with them in Heerlen, she says well, OK, I'll go with you. Because we go to Winnipeg where they moved to. And then we go from there. So in the meantime, I had-- well, I had met my-- my wife, and whatever you want to call it, in Holland-- and said, you know-- your girlfriend. And, you know, I moved over in '58. And I was here. I ask her if she wanted to come over and marry me, which she did in '59.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lammy. My wife's name is Lammy. She, in that time, in '58, I had to sign that we got-- we had to marry within 30 days. That was the Canadian law. She arrived in July. And we had to get married within 30 days. Or she had to go back to the-- to Holland again. So we got married in September-- August the 12th in 1959 here in Canada, in Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I go back for a second?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your-- the woman who looked after you support herself and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right after the war-- I guess that was even already in '44-- the Dutch government started to pay for me, because I probably was-- because I was an orphan. And, well, that was in '45 then, of course. And I was from Jewish heritage. And they have paid all along till 1958. The Dutch government has paid my studies, going to university. Every week my aunt had to go and pick up the-- the weekly allowance or whatever it was-- what she-- what she lived on. She never worked herself. So, you know, I don't know exactly what the money was. But we didn't have any real shortage of money. But I really as a boy didn't have much money, pocket money, at all. And, so, whatever I did in the summer we made money, you know, as boys-- picking cherries, working here and there, and then go on a holiday. So. But we [? lived from-- ?] the Dutch government kind of paid everything. And, you know, I had to go to university that was out of town. And so they paid my transportation. And they paid-- I was in a room there with some people, what they paid for and all this. So. Yeah, so I have no complaints about that at all. And she-- she really had, you know, lived from that, because she never did any work. However, then we got to Winnipeg in '58. And, well, but it-- you know, [INAUDIBLE] we decided-- I was gonna get married. So I started to save. So then she had to start working too. So that's when she ended up, the funny part, in Winnipeg with a family, Jewish family, helping in the-- you know, in the house, with the house work.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just gonna take a short break to change the film. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
